dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting at 3201 North Park Street, [CITY].
Hello, I'm Michael, an EMT with the rescue squad. What's going on here?
They said this man is unconscious. They've been doing CPR since he collapsed.
Can you check his pupils? His eyes aren't opening to light or touch.
GCS is 3-1 eye, 1 verbal, 1 motor.
No response to verbal or painful stimulus at all.
OPA and NPA are already in place; manual ventilation is ongoing.
Airway appears patent. No visible obstruction.
OPA and NPA in place. Ventilations being done regularly.
Confirm OPA and NPA in place with ongoing ventilation.
Assessing breathing now-rate, depth, and chest rise.
Respiratory rate is at 12 per minute.
No spontaneous chest movement; monitored as asystole.
Can you check for a pulse and blood pressure?
No pulse-0.
No blood pressure-0/0.
Pulse ox is 98%, stable for now.
He's pale, dry, and warm-no pulse. Circulation absent.
Monitor continues to show flat line-asystole.
Check his glucose; can you test it now?
Blood glucose reads 0-need to start IO access.
When was the patient last seen before collapse?
Last seen one hour ago; collapsed suddenly.
Any known medical history, like cardiac issues?
No cardiology history. He's a known recreational user but denies substance abuse.
Any surgical or past illness history to report?
No surgeries or known chronic conditions that I'm aware of.
What about last food or drink intake?
I know he was out drinking alcohol last night.
What's his name? Any other identifying details?
His name is Robert; sister is here but no ID.
No visible signs of pain or discomfort at the moment.
Assessing skin color and integrity now.
Skin is pale, dry, and warm. No signs of trauma or injury.
Check for head and neck trauma or signs.
Pupils are 3 mm and 5 mm non-reactive. No external injury.
Securing airway. Focus now on access for drug delivery.
SpO₂ has dropped to 69%. We need to investigate further.
Glucose has risen to 78. IO access is still beneficial.
Initiate intraosseous access in the humeral head.
IO access placed in left humeral head. Aspirated bone marrow.
IO line flushed with 10 ml normal saline. It's in.
Now the arm-prepare for IV access there.
IV access established at right arm. Flushed and taped down.
Give 1 mg of epinephrine 1:10,000 via IO now.
Administered epinephrine. IV line bag is running.
Supervisor's arrived-get ready for intubation and CV ECG-4 lead monitoring.
Setup complete. Supervisor visualized tracheal rings and confirmed proper bougie placement for intubation.
ET tube secured at 22 cm, capnography shows 18 mmHg. CV ECG-4 lead monitoring applied as per protocol.
Activating protocol: Cardiac Arrest-General Cardiac Protocol (Protocol 2-7).
No pulse. BP 0/0 remains. Still asystole on the monitor.
SpO₂ is at 69%. Asystoles continue on the monitor and 4 lead strip is flat line consistent with asystole.
Respiration still 12 with no spontaneous effort. Condition stable for now.
Transferring to [COMPANY_NAME]-activate lights and sirens now.
Stabilize the patient to the stretcher for transport now with three straps and rails.
Suctioned from the ET tube and nostrils after seeing a small amount of blood in the tube.
Added 5 ml of air to the tube cuff and bleeding resolved-ET tube remained secure with normal capnography at 18 mmHg.
IV bag is under pressure. ECG monitoring is stable, with clear 4 lead rhythm strip ongoing for asystole.
Second epinephrine dose-1 mg via IO, as per protocol. Given at 0813 per protocol.
Next, 2 mg of naloxone and 50 mEq of bicarb-given via IO as per standard reactivation protocol steps for arrests due to possible overdose.
Tube secure-evidence of capnography at 18 mmHg and bolus flush complete. ECG stable as monitored per 4 lead.
Three epinephrine doses given. Vitals and medications updated for hospital.
Patient secured with restraints. Three straps and both rails raised and locked.
Another 1 mg epinephrine-same route, IO. Given at 0823 per EMSA standard drug timing notes.
Complete drug regimen-1 mg EPI IO per 5-minute intervals, Narcan, bicarb. All administered per protocol.
Monitor still on asystole. Medications unchanged. 4 lead strip was flat for the entire transport period with no deviation noted in arrhythmia state.
All rigging is locked down. No movement expected during transport for patient safety and protocol adherence.
Hospital is alerted. Trauma Bay Room 1 designated per communication logs for this incident (ODEMSA - #1100).
Trachea remains clear. Last dose of EPI has been delivered in-line with cardiac arrest guidelines for co-location with VSA arrests with no ROSC seen.
IV and IO remain patent throughout full transport. All meds given in line with protocol-Narcan, EPI, bicarb given with correct timing and routes per EMSA requirements.
Full meds: 1 mg EPI per IO, 2 mg Narcan, 50 mEq bicarb with IV saline used from IO site, all in-line with IVRs and EMSA operating procedures.
Trauma Room 1 confirmed. No additional equipment required-assumed from pre-hospital staging and hospital alert with all equipment pre-staged per incident number tracking.
Same as previously noted. No other input from witness or family presented on scene at time of RAMS report.

Correct tube placement confirmed post-visualization-aligned with tracheal rings, with positive chest rise and misting during manual ventilation consistent with tracheal intubation post double-check per supervisor stdout from ED.
O2 at 15 liters per minute via NRB mask given prior and during time of ECMO maneuver during initial assessment phase of initial arrest.
Oxygen status confirmed via both monitor and visual inspection-maintained during black down and passive transport from home to roadside prior transfer into ambulance.
No response, fully unresponsive, and on full CPR. AED no shocks delivered from any defibrillation equipment including RFD AED and ambulance model
Pressure bag is on the IV bag-infusion is under controlled pressure. Normal saline running in-line with patient's IO post boluses per protocol.
Supervisor visualized tracheal rings to confirm correct tube placement via McGrath blade as per protocol narrative after initial failed esophageal intubation and successfully redirected.
No reported pain in the ePCR-documented as zero. All pain exams conducted post-intervention and showed zero, and patient's pain at rest was not reported and remained null.
